OCR does, in fact, increasingly incorporate layers of generative tools for context interpretation.
Nope -- none of these tools "interpret" anything. Neural language models do provide better word modeling and can lead to better OCR (etc) but again, that doesn't require the generative step.
Nope -- quite different. Non-generative uses of language models take in different candidate strings and rank them according to likelihood given in the training corpus. >>

SDK wired in, settings built, one line of code from going live. Then I read their fine print, and I never flipped the switch. Here is what stopped me. To get Lara's adaptive quality you have to use "Learning Mode," which stores your translations to improve their model.

Natural experiment: in Jan. 2019, Wikipedia integrated Google Translate into its Content Translation tool across 100+ language communities. The same AI shock hit communities with very different editor bases, article stocks, and readership networks.

Importantly, the quantity increase did not come with an obvious quality or demand collapse. AI-assisted translated articles maintained comparable quality/readership patterns, suggesting the human-in-the-loop workflow mattered.

At the system level, the paradox appears in knowledge flows. English became even more dominant as a source language, rising from 68% to 81% of translations, while the diversity of target languages also increased.

Editors also used AI to address representation gaps. Female biographies were translated at about twice the expected rate relative to the existing source pool, suggesting human agency can steer AI toward equity.

Importantly, additive self-labels won't cause the "puppy problem" that general post edits might. I can see some issues if someone were to then delete a late-applied label, but that should be much easier to resolve (not much different than a mod label removed after an appeal).

Many translators paint a picture of how, despite there being no revolutionary advance in automating translation, the floor began to fall out from the industry around 2023. OpenAI, it seemed, made 'good enough' translations acceptable, and demand for quality work evaporated.

I worked at a place that wanted to translate their app into different languages, and mentioned using Google Translate to do it... Besides the inaccuracy, this also didn't take into account the gendering and different layers of formality in certain languages. I was like, hella NOPE! Do it right.
"new generative AI tools are rapidly improving in quality and are good enough to competently handle routine translations of mundane texts" www.theglobeandmail.com/opinion/edit...

Some translators I spoke to said their freelance work is down 90%. Others said it's dried up completely. Others say it's only a matter of time, and are looking into new careers. One games company that had translators on staff said they could stay—if they took a 50% pay cut.

Many translators fear and lament what will be lost as their work gets automated. Many were never in it for the money, but for love of the work—of translating games, prose and art. They worry AI-generated output stands to flatten and collapse culture itself, our very ability to understand each other.

The disruption has taken a lot of translators by surprise. They have, after all, seen automated tools proposed and used to varying degrees by clients for years, even decades, before ChatGPT. Many say GPT's output isn't much better than Google Translate—so many did not see the shift coming.

LLMs like GPT were not really designed for translation. That's what Google Translate is; it's a machine learning system but not an LLM. The point the previous comment was making is that the older technology already worked about as well, so the outsized job-killing impact of LLMs is hard to justify.

What LLMs are very good at, which they *were* designed for, is producing sentences that sound like something a person would say, regardless of their meaning. So their translation output can look superficially better in some cases than other ML technologies - accurate or not.

But even in tests where people judged the LLM's work to be a bit better than Google, it wasn't so much better that you'd expect it to make the difference between "yeah we use Google for some of this" to "fire most of our translators."

I think there's a nuance missed here. Yes, LLMs like GPT don't handle translation well because it's trained to be a general purpose language processor. So it can do some translation, but you're also dealing with "noises" from the rest of the model weights.

The rapid uptake is partially because the dedicated translation models are improving rapidly due to the development of transformer architecture (the underlying architecture of LLM). Trying to use GPT performance as the metric to judge risk of AI replacement is like trying

and it is used in many scenarios (such as in emergencies/crises, for medical purposes, legal purposes) and it is used because we do not have enough translators or the tasks are not critical enough to warrant translators so how can you not say that AI is useful?

the problem is when AI is used in contexts where it does not make sense and it cannot compete with humans say you want to translate (we call it localise) a video game into another language. AI obviously cannot compete. it can't produce art, quirky jokes, use specific language that appeals to people

in this case, I am more than happy supporting the use of humans over AI, because you can't even compare the quality of a professional translator to an AI system. we just have to debate use cases properly to truly reach different answers to "is AI useful"? and this is only about translation!

there are some factors that are non-negotiables (ethics, copyright, sustainability), but then I must ask you: if a brazilian scientist who has no access to English and no funds to pay for a translator, is it ethical for them to use AI to read a journal article?

if someone seeking legal and asylum assistance, and they can't speak the language and community interpreters are not available (more common than you think!), should we get rid of AI? if someone is an immigrant and has to seek medical assistance, is using AI for help any good?

I will always vouch for humans in critical situations (medical, legal, in education, etc.), but with how the world is, if they can get any help, and if AI systems that translate can provide that, what is the harm?

(Although I do take a certain delight in writing it up as though every AI issue is a deliberate choice by the "translator". "The translator translated the abbreviation ABC in seven different ways throughout the text, all of which were incorrect.")

For Final Fantasy XI, they had planned for the English translation from the start, but the game wasn't released in NA until almost a full year after the Japanese version was released, so some localization presumably also took place after-the-fact.

Similarly, for Final Fantasy XII, Alexander O. Smith says the game was about halfway complete when they started the translation process, but that game also came out in NA about 7 months after the JP release, so the localization process presumably continued after-the-fact.

It wasn't until Final Fantasy XIII that the majority of the localization seemed to take place mid-development in an effort to achieve simultaneous worldwide shipment, which caused a lot of headaches because that was a new concept back then.

Incidentally, there were more people than Ted Woolsey who helped localize Square games in the NES and SNES days, namely: Kaoru Moriyama, Nathan Williams, Weimin Li, Aiko Ito, Rika Maruya, Noriko Wada, and more. (See their mobygames pages.)

... churning out terrible quality translations at a dismal fee that high quality translators couldn't compete against if they wanted to. But most clients knew this, and there was ample work for translators providing high quality services at a reasonable price. 4/

Gradually, though, machine translation started to become a thing. A slow but steady shift started. More and more jobs weren't straight translation anymore, but "MTPE": Machine Translation Post-Editing. 5/

Since it was "just" editing, not translation, these jobs were paid a lot less than translation, about a third of the rate per word in general, but MTPE would often take almost as much time as doing a translation from scratch would take, and with a poorer result, ... 6/
Over the past decades, this shift has gradually accelerated. High quality work has totally dried up and the world at large is now inundated with piss poor AI translations because of it. 8/

After that I'll go through and explain which translations worked best for each subtitle - what were the best approaches and why! We'll make adjustments to the subtitles as necessary, and then when we're finished, we'll have a nice set of polished subs for our scene😃

And that's it! There will be some time for Q&A at the end of the entire Open School session where you can ask any questions you may have about translation, our course, etc. It's a lot of fun, so we hope to see you there!💫

early pre-GPT LLMs were initially being researched for machine translation. It's the same technical core (transformer architecture based on text tokenization that does sequential next-word prediction).

from the perspective of linguistics, you can subscribe to different theories of language (structuralist, dialogism, functionalism, sociocultural theories) and they all uphold to different aspects (language, cognition or the social nature of humans)

(this is why it is important to be a good linguist/philosopher if you want to build good neural systems or LLMs) adopting this perspective, you know very well the limitations of your systems. you have to EMBED things such as context or world knowledge into your systems.

now, why is this relevant? knowing that my systems have their limitations, I can claim correctly the things they can and can't do. so for example, i subscribe to a philosophy more aligned with hermeneutics (interpretation as the basis of everything) we interpret because we are humans.

machines don't interpret. they may be trained on interpretations, but when the algorithms are organising, reorganising, labelling, training, etc., i know this is just computation that will be made meaningful by a human who will read whatever it will produce

in this perspective, it is obvious that AI is not sentient. like I said, you can "inject" interpretations to make AI mimic human speech patterns (in written or oral form), but that is still only a product of statistics. THIS is only the basis for how the systems work.

why is this important? because we should be using studies from the literature to adequately make claims about the usefulness and uselessness of AI systems i specialise in AI systems that translate texts from one language into another, so ideally I should only make claims about this type of use case

machines cannot engage w/ anything meaningfully. their process does not involve consideration. for practical purposes & SOME translation applications this doesn't matter. want to message a foreigner? it's great! want to translate technical documents? post-editing saves so much time & grunt work

offloading translation of art to machines meanwhile nothing but sever the connection between the artist, the work itself the people who engage with it. even if it were technically correct (whatever that means) where's the joy in reading words picked without any human thought? there's no point!

can correctly translate a "genderless pronoun" language into a "gendered pronoun" like English. The factor missing from digital translations? Deep understanding of context and culture. For us, if we cannot guess a subject's gender, we translate it in English as in our own native language: plural.
